summary: Dutch architect and architectural theorist, founder of OMA (Office of Metropolitan Architecture). Winner of the 2000 Pritzker Prize. Known for the Seattle Central Library, CCTV Headquarters in Beijing, Casa da Musica in Porto, and De Rotterdam. Also a major theorist with books Delirious New York and S,M,L,XL. Coined the term 'Junkspace' and taught at Harvard.

works.name: General / Biographical
works.description: Rem Koolhaas (full name Remment Lucas Koolhaas) is a Dutch architect who co-founded the Office of Metropolitan Architecture (OMA) with Elia Zenghelis. He studied at the Architectural Association in London, where Zaha Hadid was among his students. OMA is based in Rotterdam and has incubated notable 'starchitects' including Joshua Prince-Ramus (who went on to found REX) and Bjarke Ingels (who founded BIG). Koolhaas is a Harvard professor and winner of the 2000 Pritzker Prize. He coined the term 'Junkspace.' He collaborated with Ole Scheeren on the CCTV headquarters design. Wikipedia

works.name: Seattle Central Library
works.description: A glass-and-steel public library in Seattle, Washington, designed by Koolhaas and OMA. The building features an irregular diamond-patterned glass facade and is conceived as five stable platforms ('stability clusters') connected by four 'in between' planes. Interior features include a four-story book spiral (numbers 000–999 painted on the floor), the Mixing Chamber (connected to the Living Room by fluorescent yellow-green escalators), ring-shaped blood-red corridors connecting meeting rooms, and George Legrady's installation 'Making Visible the Invisible' (six plasma screens showing live data), and diagonal half-white/half-black support columns. Has capacity for 1.45 million items. Wikipedia

clues.clue: Mentioned as a signature Koolhaas work across nearly all questions — his most frequently cited building

this Dutch architect and winner of the 2000 Pritzker Prize [designed the Seattle Central Library]

works.name: CCTV Headquarters (Beijing)
works.description: The China Central Television Headquarters in Beijing, designed by Koolhaas and OMA in collaboration with Ole Scheeren. Features two slanted towers connected by a 75-meter perpendicular cantilevered section, forming a loop. Often nicknamed for its resemblance to a pair of shorts (or 'big pants'). Wikipedia

works.name: Delirious New York
works.description: An architectural manifesto/book by Koolhaas, subtitled 'A Retroactive Manifesto for Manhattan.' Praises the 'chance-like' nature of city living and introduces the term 'Manhattanism' as an ideology embracing urban congestion. Koolhaas called Manhattan the 'twentieth century's Rosetta Stone' in the book. The cover features his wife Madelon Vriesendorp's painting 'Flagrant Délit,' which shows two distorted skyscrapers lying in bed next to the Statue of Liberty's torch. Wikipedia

works.name: S,M,L,XL
works.description: A major theoretical book by Koolhaas, co-designed with Bruce Mau. Entries are arranged chronologically within categories named Small, Medium, Large, and Extra-Large. Contains essays including 'Islam after Einstein' and the poem 'Learning Japanese,' and describes a masterplan for Yokohama using 'programmatic lava.' Introduces the concept of the 'Architecture of Bigness.' Wikipedia

works.name: EU Flag Barcode Redesign (2002)
works.description: In 2002, Koolhaas proposed a never-adopted redesign of the European Union flag that is typically referred to by its resemblance to a barcode. The design was praised for being easily updated to reflect the entry of new member states. Wikipedia

works.name: Casa da Musica (Porto)
works.description: A concert hall in Porto, Portugal, designed by Koolhaas and OMA. Its angular white concrete form has been compared to a Jawa sandcrawler from Star Wars. Frequently cited alongside the Seattle Central Library as one of his most important buildings. Wikipedia

works.name: Maison a Bordeaux
works.description: A house in Bordeaux, France, designed by OMA in collaboration with Cecil Balmond. A notable feature is its moving platform/elevator — the house was designed for a client in a wheelchair. Wikipedia

comprehensive_summary:
Rem Koolhaas (full name Remment Lucas Koolhaas) is a Dutch architect and architectural theorist. He co-founded the Office of Metropolitan Architecture (OMA) with Elia Zenghelis; the firm is based in Rotterdam. Zaha Hadid was among his students at the Architectural Association in London. OMA has incubated prominent architects including Joshua Prince-Ramus (later founder of REX) and Bjarke Ingels (later founder of BIG). Koolhaas is a Harvard professor and won the Pritzker Prize in 2000. He coined the term 'Junkspace' in an influential essay.

His most frequently cited building is the Seattle Central Library, a glass-and-steel structure with a distinctive diamond-patterned irregular facade. The interior is organized as five stable platforms connected by four 'in between' planes. Notable features include the Mixing Chamber (connected to the Living Room by fluorescent yellow-green escalators), ring-shaped blood-red corridors, a four-story continuous book spiral (with Dewey numbers 000–999 painted on the floor), and George Legrady's installation 'Making Visible the Invisible' featuring six plasma screens displaying live data. It has capacity for 1.45 million items. Another major architectural work is the CCTV Headquarters in Beijing — designed with Ole Scheeren — which features two slanted towers connected by a 75-meter perpendicular cantilevered section and is often nicknamed for its resemblance to a pair of shorts. He also designed the Casa da Musica (concert hall) in Porto, Portugal; De Rotterdam (the largest building in the Netherlands); the Qatar National Library in Doha (completed 2017); the McCormick Tribune Campus Center at IIT (topped by a tubular metal train tunnel); the Shenzhen Stock Exchange; the RAK Convention Center in the UAE (resembling the Death Star); the Guggenheim Hermitage Museum in Las Vegas; and the Maison a Bordeaux (designed with Cecil Balmond as a moving house for a wheelchair-bound client). OMA also created galleries and a 'Transformer' building for Prada in Milan and Seoul, and worked on the Serpentine Gallery pavilion in 2006.

As a theorist, Koolhaas wrote Delirious New York, which praises the 'chance-like' nature of city living and introduces the term 'Manhattanism' — an ideology embracing urban congestion. He called Manhattan the 'twentieth century's Rosetta Stone.' The book's cover features his wife Madelon Vriesendorp's painting 'Flagrant Delit,' depicting two distorted skyscrapers in bed next to the Statue of Liberty's torch. He co-authored the large-format theoretical book S,M,L,XL with designer Bruce Mau; entries are arranged chronologically within categories Small, Medium, Large, and Extra-Large. The book contains essays including 'Islam after Einstein' and the poem 'Learning Japanese,' describes a Yokohama masterplan as 'programmatic lava,' and develops the concept of the 'Architecture of Bigness.' In 2002, he proposed a never-adopted barcode redesign of the EU flag, praised for its ability to easily incorporate new member states.
